title: Stock Teaching
subtitle: Questions & answers (beginner → advanced)
audienceNote: Plain-language explanations for high school readers. Educational only — not financial advice.

sequence.levelSummary: What stocks are, why people invest, and how the market behaves over long periods.

items.question: In one sentence, what is a share of stock?
items.answer: A share is a small slice of ownership in a company. If the company does well over time, your slice can become more valuable.
items.example: Imagine you and nine friends open a lemonade stand and split it 10 ways. Each person’s paper slip is like one share — you own part of the business.

items.question: Why do companies sell stock to the public?
items.answer: They sell pieces of ownership to raise money so they can grow — hire people, build factories, invent products, or pay down debt.
items.example: A bike company needs $1 million for a new factory. Instead of borrowing it all from a bank, it sells shares so many investors chip in.

items.question: What does it mean that the stock market has “gone up on average” over many years?
items.answer: It means that, measured over long stretches (like decades), broad groups of stocks have tended to grow faster than cash sitting in a drawer — even though there are crashes along the way.
items.example: Think of a bumpy road that still climbs a hill: short parts go down (crashes), but the long trip is upward if you zoom out far enough.

items.question: What is inflation, in simple words?
items.answer: Inflation is when money buys less over time because prices for food, rent, and other stuff slowly rise.
items.example: If a movie ticket was $8 last year and $9 this year, your same $8 buys less fun than before.

items.question: Why can keeping all your savings as cash for decades be risky?
items.answer: Because inflation can shrink what your money can buy, even if the number on your account stays the same.
items.example: $1,000 under your mattress is still $1,000 — but years later it might only buy what $600 used to buy.

items.question: What is the basic difference between saving and investing?
items.answer: Saving is parking money somewhere safer and steadier (like a bank account). Investing means taking risk to try to grow money over time, usually by buying assets like stocks or funds.
items.example: Savings account: small, steady interest. Stocks: bigger possible growth, but the value can swing up and down a lot.

items.question: Do you need to be rich to start learning about investing?
items.answer: No. Many people start small and learn habits first — regular amounts, patience, and not betting money they need for rent or food.
items.example: Like practicing piano 10 minutes a day: small, steady steps beat waiting for a “perfect” moment.

items.question: What does “long-term” usually mean for stock investing?
items.answer: It means measuring results in years or decades, not days — giving good companies and diversified baskets time to ride out bad years.
items.example: Planting a tree: you do not dig it up every week to check roots. You water it and wait seasons.

items.question: What is a stock market crash?
items.answer: A fast, scary drop in prices across many stocks. It hurts in the moment, but history shows markets have recovered after major crashes — though it can take time.
items.example: Like a sudden thunderstorm on a hike: unpleasant, but not the same as the trail disappearing forever.

items.question: Why is copying “whatever is hype on the internet today” a bad plan for beginners?
items.answer: Because hype moves fast and emotions run hot. Beginners do better building boring basics: learn terms, diversify, think in years, and avoid gambling with money you need.
items.example: Chasing a viral stock tip is like buying concert tickets from a stranger in a parking lot — sometimes fine, often a mess.

sequence.levelSummary: Funds, diversification, habits, and why emotions matter as much as numbers.

items.question: What is an index, like the S&P 500, in plain English?
items.answer: A scoreboard that tracks a big group of companies. The S&P 500 follows roughly 500 large U.S. companies — not one stock, a whole basket.
items.example: Instead of grading one student, you average the grades of 500 students to see how “the whole grade” is doing.

items.question: What is an ETF (simple version)?
items.answer: A fund you can buy and sell like a stock, which usually holds many companies or bonds inside one ticker — a handy way to diversify.
items.example: A smoothie: many fruits blended together. One cup, many ingredients.

items.question: What is diversification?
items.answer: Not putting all your money in one company or one bet. If one part struggles, other parts can balance the ride.
items.example: A sports team: one injured player hurts less if the rest of the roster is strong.

items.question: Why does starting earlier often matter more than the exact stock you pick?
items.answer: Because time lets compounding (growth on growth) work. Starting late means you miss years of potential snowballing.
items.example: Two snowballs rolling downhill: the one that starts higher on the hill ends much bigger, even with the same snow.

items.question: What is compound growth in one picture?
items.answer: Your money earns returns, and then those returns can earn returns too — like a snowball picking up more snow as it rolls.
items.example: $100 grows 8% to $108. Next year 8% applies to $108, not just the original $100.

items.question: Why do many teachers say “investing is emotional,” not just math?
items.answer: Because fear and greed make people sell low after drops or chase hype at the top. Staying calm is a skill.
items.example: Panic-selling after a bad quiz can make you quit school; zooming out helps you recover and learn.

items.question: What is a simple way to think about risk?
items.answer: The chance you lose money or sleep badly because prices swing. More possible reward often comes with more possible pain.
items.example: Roller coasters: bigger drops, bigger thrills — not everyone wants that ride with lunch money.

items.question: What does it mean to “do your own research” (DYOR)?
items.answer: Read what the company does, how it makes money, and what could go wrong — instead of trusting a random caption online.
items.example: Before adopting a pet, you read about food, space, and vet costs — same idea for a stock.

items.question: Why is a brokerage account not the same as a bank account?
items.answer: A brokerage is where you place orders to buy/sell investments. Money and investments there face market risk — it is not the same as FDIC-insured cash rules (varies by country and product).
items.example: Bank vault for cash vs. workshop where you build a bookshelf — different tools, different risks.

sequence.levelSummary: Sectors, volatility, picking themes, and thinking about what “expensive” means.

items.question: What is a stock sector?
items.answer: A label that groups similar businesses — technology, health care, finance, energy, and so on.
items.example: Mall map: “food court,” “fashion,” “electronics.” Sectors sort companies by what they mainly do.

items.question: What is volatility?
items.answer: How wildly prices bounce up and down. High volatility means bigger mood swings on the chart.
items.example: A calm bus ride vs. a jet coaster — both get you somewhere, but one jolts you more.

items.question: What is the P/E ratio, in kid-friendly terms?
items.answer: Price divided by yearly earnings per share — a rough “how many years of profit am I paying for?” shortcut. It is not perfect, but it helps compare similar companies.
items.example: If a lemonade stand earns $10 a year and someone pays $100 for it, that is like a P/E of 10 — very rough math.

items.question: Why might a “hot stock list” still be a bad match for you?
items.answer: Because your goals, timeline, and nerves matter. A stock can be exciting and still too risky for your situation.
items.example: A race car is awesome — not everyone should daily-drive it to school.

items.question: What is dollar-cost averaging (simple idea)?
items.answer: Investing a fixed amount on a schedule so you sometimes buy high and sometimes low — smoothing out the urge to time the market perfectly.
items.example: Buying one pizza slice every Friday: some weeks it is on sale, some weeks full price — average evens out.

items.question: What is a dividend (basic definition)?
items.answer: Cash some companies pay shareholders from profits, usually on a schedule. Not all companies pay dividends.
items.example: A club shares some snack money with members when the year goes well.

items.question: Why is comparing yourself to influencers dangerous in investing?
items.answer: You rarely see their full picture — losses, debt, or sponsorships. Your race is your timeline and budget.
items.example: Comparing your first 5K run to someone’s edited highlight reel.

items.question: What does “market cap” mean?
items.answer: Roughly: share price × shares outstanding. It estimates the total value the market puts on the whole company.
items.example: If 1 million shares exist at $20 each, market cap is about $20 million — a yardstick for size, not guaranteed truth.

items.question: Why read both bull and bear arguments before buying?
items.answer: So you understand what could go wrong, not only the hype story. Good decisions weigh upside and downside.
items.example: Buying a phone: read 5-star and 1-star reviews, not only the ad.

sequence.levelSummary: Day trading, strategy discipline, risk/reward, and why most short-term traders struggle.

items.question: What is day trading in one line?
items.answer: Opening and closing trades within the same day (often many times) to try to profit from short price moves — very different from long-term investing.
items.example: Surfing one wave all morning vs. sailing across an ocean — different sport, different injuries.

items.question: Why do educators say most day traders should expect a steep learning curve?
items.answer: Because short-term prices are noisy, fees add up, emotions spike, and luck can fool you until it stops.
items.example: Winning a few carnival games does not mean you own the carnival.

items.question: What is a stop-loss (simple idea)?
items.answer: An order to exit a trade near a chosen price to cap how much you lose if the market moves against you. It does not guarantee a fill at exactly that price in fast markets.
items.example: Seatbelt: it does not prevent every injury, but it limits how bad the crash can get.

items.question: What is risk/reward ratio in trading talk?
items.answer: Comparing how much you might lose on a trade to how much you hope to gain. Pros often talk about small, controlled losses and bigger planned wins — but real life is messy.
items.example: Betting $1 to try to win $2 is different from betting $10 to win $1.

items.question: What is backtesting?
items.answer: Running a strategy on past charts/data to see how it would have performed. Useful to learn mechanics — but the future is not a replay of the past.
items.example: Practicing a play against old game tape: helpful drill, not a promise of next Friday’s game.

items.question: Why can “more screen time” hurt traders?
items.answer: Overtrading, revenge trades after losses, and ignoring a plan. Rules and breaks matter.
items.example: Studying 16 hours straight before a test — tired brains make sloppy mistakes.

items.question: What is a fair takeaway from long-term investing teachers like the “15 minutes of lessons” style videos?
items.answer: Principles repeat: patience, understanding the business, managing emotions, and avoiding arrogance after wins.
items.example: Great coaches repeat basics — layups, breathing, footwork — not because pros are dumb, because discipline beats tricks.

items.question: If you remember only three rules before risking real money, what should they be?
items.answer: (1) Never use money you need for bills soon. (2) Understand what you buy. (3) Know that you can lose — so size your bets and sleep at night.
items.example: Helmet, lights, brakes — before a night bike ride. Boring saves you.
